summary: DHH reflects on the disconnect between people who admire Basecamp's philosophy, books, and Rails but have never actually tried the Basecamp product. He acknowledges this is partly their own failure to draw the connection and ask directly. Rather than relying on surveillance-capitalism-style ad targeting, Basecamp has historically relied on goodwill and word of mouth, but DHH admits they've taken that for granted. The post ends with a direct, humble ask for readers to try Basecamp.
key_points: People who love your philosophy won't automatically try your product — you have to explicitly ask them
Longevity breeds a false assumption that awareness equals adoption
Basecamp rejects invasive ad targeting but hasn't adequately activated the goodwill they've built
There's an unsolved tension between authentic promotion and coming off as a self-promoting shill
money_quotes: If you want people to give your product a chance, you gotta ask them.
We've failed to do the work; failed to draw the connection.
Part of it is the lazy assumption that since you've been around for a long time, then of course people who like your philosophy or perspective would know what you make, and would have given it a try.
They ask a bunch of strangers to try their product that they've targeted through the mechanisms of surveillance capitalism.
You have to activate that goodwill, if you want it to translate into sales that sustain all that making, sharing, and teaching.
How do you ask people who might not be your friends, but don't feel like strangers either, if they'd try your product?
key_insight: Building an audience through philosophy and goodwill doesn't automatically convert to product adoption — you still have to directly ask people to try what you make.
